degradationCallout.title: Early warning alerts available
degradationCallout.description: UPG Monitor can learn this site's normal response time and alert you when it degrades significantly — before the site actually goes down.
degradationCallout.details: After 20 successful checks we learn your baseline (P75 from older checks). If the last 7 checks average at least ~2.9× that baseline (and at least ~450 ms) for 5 checks in a row, you get one warning email. Another email only happens after times settle clearly back near baseline — brief recoveries in the middle no longer re-trigger alerts.
degradationCallout.requiresEmail: Requires email alerts — enable them in the monitor's edit settings first.

degradationFormHint.addReminder: You chose to set up slow-response alerts later. Turn on "Alert on slow response times" below (under Notifications) once email alerts are enabled.
degradationFormHint.editReminder: You chose to set this up later. Turn on "Alert on slow response times" below once email alerts are enabled.

addMonitorFlow.intro: Start with one monitor when you want custom checks, or import a list when every site should share the same basic settings.
addMonitorFlow.singleDescription: Set the check type, target, alerts, and status page visibility for one service.
addMonitorFlow.singleMeta: Best for APIs, apps, DNS, and TCP checks
addMonitorFlow.bulkDescription: Paste URLs or upload a file, review the list, then apply one shared configuration.

monitorTypes.dnsDescription: Validate a hostname resolves to the expected record.
monitorTypes.tcpDescription: Check that a host and port accept connections.

monitorTypes.typeReadOnly: Monitor type cannot be changed after creation

monitorForm.name: Name
monitorForm.urlHint: Must be a valid HTTP or HTTPS URL.
monitorForm.headersRedactedHint: Sensitive headers are redacted from exports.
monitorForm.slowResponseAlertsHint: Learns this site's normal response time over 20+ checks, then alerts when a sustained 2x slowdown is detected. Fires once per episode.
monitorForm.requiresEmailAlerts: Requires email alerts to be enabled.
monitorForm.sslMonitoringHint: Checks cert validity and expiry on every run. Alerts fire when the cert becomes invalid or has 30 days or less left.
monitorForm.maintenanceSuppressesAlerts: Alerts are suppressed while the window is active.
monitorForm.statusPageHint: When enabled, this monitor appears at
monitorForm.basicsHint: Choose what to monitor and give it a recognizable name.
monitorForm.checkSettingsHint: Defaults are tuned for normal uptime checks. Adjust them only when this monitor needs stricter timing or response rules.
monitorForm.notificationsHint: Decide who should hear about failures and slowdowns.
monitorForm.maintenanceWindowHint: Optional. Use this when a planned change should suppress alerts for a short window.

bulkEdit.noChanges: Change at least one setting, or cancel.
bulkEdit.updatedCount: {count, plural, one {# monitor updated} other {# monitors updated}}
bulkEdit.scopeHint: Name and URL stay per monitor. Only the settings below are applied to all selected.
bulkEdit.slowResponseAlertsHint: Learns each site's normal response time over 20+ checks, then alerts when a sustained 2x slowdown is detected. Fires once per episode.
bulkEdit.sslBulkHint: Applies to HTTPS monitors. HTTP-only monitors ignore this.

account.subtitle: Manage your account details and security settings.

account.securitySubtitle: Change your password. You'll stay signed in after changing it.
account.usernameHelp: 2+ characters. Used to sign in instead of email.
account.apiSubtitle: Create and manage API keys for external dashboards and widgets.
account.apiPublicHint: Public endpoint is still available at /api/status/{username}.
account.apiTokenShownOnce: This token is shown only once. Save it now.
account.apiExamplesIntro: Send your API key as a Bearer token. Replace YOUR_API_TOKEN with the secret shown when you create or rotate a key.
account.apiExampleCorsHint: Browser widgets: add your site’s origin (for example https://app.example.com) to Allowed origins on the key, or the browser will block the request (CORS).
account.apiExampleQueryHint: Optional query: ?monitorId='<id>' limits to one monitor; ?limit=100 caps how many monitors are returned (default 100, max 500).
account.apiResponseFieldsHint: Each monitor in data.monitors includes type, intervalMinutes, paused, method, SSL fields, consecutiveFailures, uptimePct and checkCount90d (90 days), plus status timestamps.
account.statusPageTabIntro: Configure your public URL (you need a username in Profile), how the page reads, the footer, and which monitors are visible to visitors.
account.statusPageMonitorsSubtitle: Only checked monitors appear on your public page. Others stay in your account only.
account.statusPageMonitorsEmpty: You don’t have any monitors yet.
account.statusPageTitleHelp: Shown as the main heading. If empty, your username is used.
account.statusPageTaglineHelp: Optional subtitle below the title (for example team or product name).
account.statusPageShowPoweredBy: Show “Powered by UPG Monitor” in the footer
account.statusPageNeedUsername: Set a username on the Profile tab to get a public URL, then you can customize this page.

help.pageSubtitle: Documentation for UPG Monitor features. Search across the guide, jump by topic, or start with the common support links below.
help.searchEmpty: No articles match your search. Try different words or clear the search.
help.supportBody: Start with the topics operators use most: add a monitor, tune alerts, publish status, connect an external dashboard, or move data between installs.

welcome.title: Welcome to UPG Monitor
welcome.summary: What this app does and how monitors work.
body: UPG Monitor checks URLs, hosts, ports, DNS records, keywords, and certificates on a schedule. Each run records whether the check succeeded, how long it took, and any status details needed for later review.
Your monitors are private by default. You can publish selected monitors on a read-only status page, share data through the status API, and keep operational history inside your account.
Use Dashboard for the current picture, Monitors for configuration, Activity for cross-monitor events, and Help & Support when you need a reference.

signInAndAccount.title: Signing in and registration
signInAndAccount.summary: Login, setup, and who can use the app.
body: Sign in with your email or username and password. If the server was just installed, an administrator creates the first account on the setup page; after that, new users may register if registration is enabled.
Your session keeps you signed in until you sign out or the session expires. You can update profile details and change your password from Account.

firstRunSetup.title: First-run setup
firstRunSetup.summary: How a new instance becomes ready for users.
body: A new installation starts with setup mode. The first administrator creates the initial account, then the app switches into normal sign-in and registration behavior.
If setup has already been completed, the setup page is disabled. Registration availability after setup depends on the server configuration.

onboardingGuide.title: Onboarding guide
onboardingGuide.summary: A short product tour you can reopen.
body: The onboarding guide from the account menu walks you through the product in a short overlay flow. It is useful when you are new to the app or showing someone the main workflow.
Help & Support is the deeper reference. Use it when you need exact behavior for checks, alerts, status pages, exports, or admin tools.

dashboardOverview.title: Dashboard overview
dashboardOverview.summary: Summary cards and monitor status at a glance.
body: The Dashboard shows how many monitors are up, down, or paused, and lists each monitor with its current state.
Paused monitors are not checked until you resume them from the Monitors page. New accounts see an empty state that points to adding the first monitor.
Dashboard data comes from stored monitor and check results, so it is the best first place to confirm what is happening right now.

dashboardSearchAndStatusLink.title: Dashboard search and status page link
dashboardSearchAndStatusLink.summary: Find monitors quickly and open your public page.
body: Use the search field to filter monitors by name or URL. Clear the search to return to the full list.
If your account has a username, the status page link opens /status/your-username so you can review what visitors see.

dashboardTrends.title: Trend charts
dashboardTrends.summary: Recent check history on the Dashboard.
body: Trend charts show recent response patterns without opening the full monitor detail page. They help you spot flapping, slowdowns, or a sudden run of failed checks.
New monitors may have sparse charts until enough scheduled or manual checks have been recorded.

addingMonitors.title: Adding a monitor
addingMonitors.summary: Create a new check from Dashboard or Monitors.
body: Choose Add monitor, enter a clear name, and provide the target details for the monitor type. HTTP and keyword monitors use a full HTTP or HTTPS URL; DNS and TCP monitors use host-oriented fields.
Each monitor runs independently on its configured interval. You can later edit the schedule, timeout, notifications, status page visibility, and advanced request behavior.
bullets: Use descriptive names so alerts and activity entries are easy to understand.
Start with a conservative interval and timeout, then tighten them after you understand the service behavior.
Enable status page visibility only for monitors you want visitors or customers to see.

monitorTypes.title: Monitor types
monitorTypes.summary: HTTP, keyword, DNS, and TCP checks.
body: HTTP monitors check whether a web endpoint returns an expected status code before the timeout. Keyword monitors fetch the response body and verify that expected text is present or absent.
DNS monitors resolve a hostname and can compare record values. TCP monitors verify that a host and port accept a connection.
Choose the type that matches the failure you care about. For example, use keyword checks when a page can return 200 while still showing an error message.

monitorSettings.title: Monitor settings explained
monitorSettings.summary: Interval, timeout, method, status codes, alerts, SSL, and status page visibility.
body: Interval is how often checks run. Timeout is the maximum wait before the check is treated as failed. Expected status codes define which HTTP responses count as success.
HTTP method can be GET or HEAD where supported. Keyword monitors always use GET because they need the response body.
Email alerts notify you when a monitor goes down or recovers. SSL monitoring checks certificate validity and expiry. Show on status page controls whether the monitor appears publicly.

advancedRequestSettings.title: Advanced request settings
advancedRequestSettings.summary: Headers, body, redirects, and request customization.
body: Advanced HTTP settings let you add request headers, choose a body type, send a request body, and control redirect behavior. Use these when a health endpoint needs authentication headers, form data, or a non-default redirect policy.
Sensitive headers are redacted from monitor exports. Keep secrets scoped to the monitored endpoint and rotate them if an exported file is shared too widely.

monitorsListAndBulk.title: Monitors list, sorting, and bulk actions
monitorsListAndBulk.summary: Table controls, selection, pause, resume, edit, and delete.
body: The Monitors table supports sorting by fields such as name, last check, and interval. Search narrows the list by name or URL.
Select one or more monitors to pause, resume, edit shared fields in bulk, or delete. Bulk edit applies only the fields you choose and leaves per-monitor details like name and URL alone.
Deleting a monitor removes its configuration and history from your account. Treat delete as permanent.

importExportMonitors.title: Import and export monitors
importExportMonitors.summary: JSON backup and restore for monitor definitions.
body: Export downloads a JSON file of monitor definitions for backup or copying to another environment.
Import accepts a JSON array of monitor objects. Review the preview and confirm; invalid entries are reported without stopping the whole import when possible.

monitorDetailPage.title: Monitor detail page
monitorDetailPage.summary: History, charts, check now, edit, and delete.
body: Open a monitor from Dashboard or the Monitors table to inspect detailed history, response times, SSL information, incident context, and configuration actions.
Check now runs an immediate check outside the normal schedule. Edit and Delete apply only to the current monitor.
Use the detail page when a summary status is not enough and you need exact timing, response, or certificate evidence.

checkResultsAndIncidents.title: Check results and incidents
body: Check results show individual runs, including status, response time, and error details when available. Incidents group meaningful down and recovery periods so you can understand when a problem started and ended.
The app confirms failures before declaring a monitor down, which reduces noise from brief network blips. Recovery is recorded when successful checks resume.

emailAlerts.title: Email alerts
body: Email alerts send a message when a monitor changes state, such as going down or recovering. They are designed around transitions so you do not receive repeated messages for the same ongoing outage.
A monitor can use the account email or a monitor-specific alert address. Email delivery still depends on the instance SMTP configuration.

sslAlerts.title: SSL certificate alerts
sslAlerts.summary: Validity and expiry checks for HTTPS monitors.
body: When SSL monitoring is enabled, HTTPS monitors check certificate validity and expiry during regular runs.
Alerts fire when the certificate becomes invalid or has 30 days or less remaining, giving you time to renew before visitors are affected.

degradationAlerts.title: Slow-response alerts
degradationAlerts.summary: Early warnings before a service goes fully down.
body: Slow-response alerts learn a monitor's normal response time after enough successful checks. When recent checks stay significantly slower than that baseline, the app can send one warning email for the episode.
These alerts require email alerts. They are useful for APIs and pages where latency problems matter even before requests fail.

maintenanceWindows.title: Maintenance windows
body: A maintenance window marks planned downtime or deployments for a monitor. While the window is active, alerts are suppressed so expected failures do not create noise.
Use the note field to record why the window exists, such as a deployment or provider maintenance event.

downtimeAcknowledgement.title: Downtime acknowledgement
downtimeAcknowledgement.summary: Mark an incident as seen from the app or email.
body: Downtime acknowledgement controls let you mark a monitor's current downtime as seen. Email acknowledgement links can also be used when the instance is configured to include them.
Acknowledgement helps operators coordinate response. It does not make a failed monitor healthy; the monitor must recover through successful checks.

activityFeed.title: Activity feed
activityFeed.summary: Recent up and down events across monitors.
body: Activity lists recent status changes across monitors, with pagination for browsing older entries.
The Activity navigation indicator can show unread incident-related updates. Clear or dismiss controls affect what you see in the feed, not the underlying monitor configuration.
Use Activity when you need a chronological story across all monitors instead of detail for one monitor.

accountProfile.title: Profile and language
accountProfile.summary: Username, email, role, and interface language.
body: Account shows your profile details, role, and language preference. You can change your username and interface language; email is not editable after registration.
Your username is also used for the public status page URL, so choose one that is safe to share if you plan to publish status.

accountSecurity.title: Password and security
body: Change password requires your current password and a new password that meets the app requirements. After a successful change, you remain signed in.
If you suspect a password or API key was exposed, update the password and rotate or revoke affected API keys from the API tab.

accountExportImport.title: Account export and import
accountExportImport.summary: Full account backup including monitors and optional history.
body: From Account you can export a JSON file with your profile, monitors, and optionally check history for archival or migration.
Import can merge data or restore in replace mode depending on the options you choose. Read the on-screen descriptions carefully before confirming.
Large histories increase file size. Export without check results when you only need configuration.

monitorExportImport.title: Monitor-only export and import
monitorExportImport.summary: Move monitor definitions without the rest of the account.
body: Monitor export/import is useful when you want to copy checks between accounts or environments without moving the full account profile and history.
Imported monitors still need valid settings for the current instance, including reachable targets, supported monitor types, and alert addresses that make sense for the destination.

publicStatusPage.title: Public status page
publicStatusPage.summary: Sharing uptime with visitors without a login.
body: Your public page lives at /status/your-username. Only monitors with Show on status page enabled appear there.
Visitors do not need to sign in. The page shows high-level status and history suitable for customers, teammates, or stakeholders.
Keep private internal checks hidden by leaving their public visibility off.

statusPageCustomization.title: Customizing the status page
statusPageCustomization.summary: Title, tagline, footer, and monitor visibility.
body: Under Account -> Status page, you can set the public page title, tagline, footer preference, and which monitors are visible.
You need a username before the public URL is available. After saving, open the public page to confirm the copy and monitor list look right.

statusWidgetApi.title: Status widget API
statusWidgetApi.summary: Read monitor status from external dashboards.
body: The widget/status API returns monitor status data for external dashboards and embeds. Use it when another site needs a machine-readable status summary.
For authenticated widget access, create an API key with the status:read scope and configure allowed origins for browser-based calls.

apiKeys.title: API keys
apiKeys.summary: Create, rotate, revoke, and use status API tokens.
body: The Account API tab lets you create API keys for external dashboards and widgets. Keys are shown only once when created or rotated, so copy the secret immediately.
Send the key as a Bearer token. Optional query parameters can limit results to one monitor or cap the number of monitors returned.
Rotate a key when you need a new secret. Revoke keys that are unused, exposed, or no longer needed.

apiKeySecurity.title: API key security and CORS
apiKeySecurity.summary: Scopes, origins, expiry, and safe sharing.
body: API keys use scopes. Status integrations should use status:read so the token can read status data without broader account access.
For browser widgets, add each allowed site origin to the key. Without an allowed origin, browsers block the request through CORS.
Use expiration dates for temporary integrations and avoid committing tokens to source control or public documentation.

themeAndLanguage.title: Theme and language
body: Use the theme control in the header to switch light and dark mode. Your choice is remembered on this device.
Interface language is set on the Account page and applies across the signed-in experience.

pwaOffline.title: Installed app, offline, and refresh
body: You can install UPG Monitor as a PWA from a supporting browser for a more app-like shortcut.
When you are offline, you may see an offline page until connectivity returns. Pull-to-refresh on supported mobile browsers reloads the current view when you are back online.

adminOverview.title: Admin overview
adminOverview.summary: Who admin is for and what the admin area contains.
body: Users with the admin role see an Admin link in the navigation. The admin area is for operating the whole instance, not just personal monitors.
The overview shows aggregate statistics such as user count, monitor count, and recent check volume.

adminUsers.title: Managing users (admin)
body: Admins can list users and perform administrative actions available for the deployment, such as reviewing access or role-related settings.
Treat admin capabilities carefully because they affect other people's accounts.

adminMonitorsAndSettings.title: Admin monitors and settings (admin)
body: Admins may inspect monitors and application settings across the installation.
Use the admin area for instance-level work. Normal monitor ownership and day-to-day edits still belong in Dashboard, Monitors, and Account.

landing.subheadline: UPG Monitor checks your URLs and APIs every minute. When something changes, you know before your users do.
landing.heroReassurance: Setup in minutes, no complex onboarding.
landing.panelFooter: Last checked 23 s ago · 60 s interval
landing.feature1Body: Every monitor runs on a 60-second cycle. HTTP status codes, response times, and SSL — checked without gaps.
landing.feature2Body: Notifications fire exactly once per state change — no spam when a service is flapping. Up is quiet; down is loud.
landing.feature3Body: Each monitor gets a shareable status page. Give your team or customers a live view — no login required.
landing.whyTitle: Downtime is silent until it’s too late.
landing.why1Title: Your users know before you do.
landing.why1Body: By the time a complaint lands in your inbox, many more visitors have already bounced. The gap between outage and awareness is where you lose people.
landing.why2Title: Every minute has a measurable cost.
landing.why2Body: Downtime during peak hours means failed checkouts, dropped signups, and lost payments — happening in real time while you’re looking at something else.
landing.why3Body: You can’t fix what you don’t know is broken. The faster the alert reaches you, the faster your users get back on track — and the less damage there is to explain.
landing.feature4Body: Detects sustained response-time slowdowns before they become outages. Learns each site’s normal baseline, then sends one warning when latency stays well above normal across several checks; the episode only ends after a clear recovery, so your inbox is not flooded by repeat alerts.

monitorDetail.nextCheckPaused: Next check: paused — no scheduled runs
monitorDetail.nextCheckPending: Next check: first run when the scheduler picks up this monitor
monitorDetail.downtimeAckEmailExpired: This link no longer matches the current outage (already recovered or changed).
monitorDetail.historySubtitle: Select a time range to view chart history.
monitorDetail.checkLogEmpty: No checks yet. Results appear after the cron job runs.
monitorDetail.chartNoData: No check data yet. Charts will appear after the cron job runs.
monitorDetail.chartUptimeLastHourOnly: Last hour only (other charts use the selected range)
monitorDetail.chartUptimeEmptyHour: No checks in the last hour.
monitorDetail.chartUptimeGroupedHint: Grouped into time segments so the bar fits the card
monitorDetail.chartResponseTrendSubAverages: Average response time per time window (smoothed).
monitorDetail.chartSummaryResponse: Successful response times: average {avg} ms, fastest {fastest} ms, slowest {slowest} ms.
monitorDetail.chartSummaryNoResponse: No successful response time data in the selected range.
monitorDetail.chartTimelineSubAverages: One point per window — average latency when up; red marks a window with any failure.
monitorDetail.chartTimelineSubFull: Each dot is one check — red dots mark failures.
monitorDetail.chartBaselineSub: P75 baseline (40 checks) compared to last 7-check average

installBanner.title: Add to Home Screen
installBanner.bodyAndroid: Install UPG Monitor for a faster, app-like experience.
installBanner.bodyIos: Tap the Share button, then "Add to Home Screen".
